## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)

**Which flow and why:** I'm showing an indexed `select(users, Eq("age", 30))` because it touches the orchestrator, the planner, the predicate Strategy, the index Strategy, and the table — and it's where "use the index" actually fires at runtime.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor Caller
    participant DS as DatabaseService
    participant T as Table
    participant P as Eq (Predicate)
    participant IX as HashIndex

    Caller->>DS: select("users", Eq("age",30))
    DS->>P: columns()
    P-->>DS: {"age"}
    Note over DS: validate "age" is in schema ✓
    DS->>P: eq_conditions()
    P-->>DS: [("age", 30)]
    DS->>T: index_for("age")
    T-->>DS: HashIndex(age)
    DS->>IX: lookup(30)
    IX-->>DS: {1, 2}  (a COPY)
    Note over DS: plan = "index(age)"
    loop for rid in sorted({1,2})
        DS->>T: row(rid)
        T-->>DS: Row(rid, values)
        DS->>P: matches(values)
        P-->>DS: True
    end
    DS-->>Caller: [{...ana}, {...ben}]
```

**🎙️ Script — arrow by arrow:** "The caller calls `select` with `Eq('age',30)`. `DatabaseService` doesn't filter anything itself — first it asks the predicate `columns()` to validate against the schema, then asks `eq_conditions()`; the `Eq` answers `[('age',30)]`. That's the interesting moment: the service asks the `Table` for an index on `age`, gets the `HashIndex`, and calls `lookup(30)` — an O(1) hit returning a *copy* of the bucket `{1,2}`. The plan is now `index(age)`. Then for each candidate id, the service fetches the row and calls `matches()` on the predicate to confirm — for a plain `Eq` it's always true, but this re-check is what lets an `And` resolve one condition via the index and verify the rest. Finally the service projects and returns."

**Why the sequence shows the design is good:** Notice the `DatabaseService` coordinates but implements no business logic — matching is delegated to the `Predicate`, the lookup to the `Index`, row storage to the `Table`. The planner's decision is driven entirely by what the predicate *advertises*, so a new operator or index type changes nothing in this flow. That's Open/Closed and Information Expert at runtime.

## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility

**DEFAULT: DESCRIBE the solution. Write code only if the interviewer asks.**

🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the exact race:** "The problem is in `insert`. It does three things that must be one step: `_next_id += 1` (a read-modify-write), write `_rows[rid]`, and `index.add(...)` for each index. Two threads can both read `_next_id == 5`, both write `_rows[6]` — a lost row. Or one thread stores the row and is paused before `index.add`, and a reader sees a row that isn't in the index. This is check-then-act / lost-update — the steps aren't atomic."

🗣️ **Step 2 — State the solution:** "I add a per-table lock. `insert`/`update`/`delete`/`select` acquire the table's lock before touching shared state and release after. The increment-store-index block becomes atomic; concurrent inserts serialize on that one table."

🗣️ **Step 3 — Trade-off:** "I lock per-table (lock striping), not one global lock — writers on different tables never block each other; only writers on the SAME table serialize, and that's microseconds. I keep type validation OUTSIDE the lock so a rejected insert never contends."

🗣️ **Step 4 — Distributed case:** "For multiple processes/servers, an in-process lock protects only one process. I'd push atomicity to the store: the id from a DB sequence, and inserts/index updates inside one DB transaction (or a compare-and-set), so the 'who gets the next id' decision is atomic across servers."

**CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY**

| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
|---|---|---|
| **Race condition** | Two threads touch shared state at once; result depends on timing | `_next_id += 1` + index writes in `insert` |
| **Critical section** | The block that must run atomically | increment → store row → add to each index |
| **Check-then-act / lost update** | Read a value, act on it, but it changed in between | two inserts both reading `_next_id == 5` |
| **Atomic** | All-or-nothing; no half-done state observable | what the per-table lock gives the insert block |
| **Mutex / lock** | A token only one thread holds at a time | `threading.RLock` per table |
| **Reentrant (RLock)** | The SAME thread can re-acquire a lock it holds | `insert_many` holds the lock and calls `insert`, which re-takes it |
| **Lock granularity** | How much one lock covers — coarse vs fine | per-TABLE (striped), not one global lock |
| **Lock striping** | Many small locks keyed by resource | `dict[str, RLock]` keyed by table name |
| **Pessimistic locking** | Grab the lock before touching state | the in-process `RLock` approach |
| **Optimistic locking** | Don't lock; write conditionally, retry on conflict | the distributed DB compare-and-set |
| **Compare-and-set (CAS)** | Atomic "update only if value still X" | `WHERE id=? AND status=?`-style guarded write |
| **Deadlock** | Two threads each hold a lock the other needs | avoided: we hold only ONE table lock at a time |

The candidate sentence: "This is a lost-update race in `insert`'s critical section; I take a pessimistic per-table mutex — reentrant because `insert_many` re-enters via `insert` — using lock striping so unrelated tables don't contend; for the distributed case I switch to optimistic locking with a DB sequence + CAS."

**CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A**

> ❓ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?"
> "Because a locked method calls another locked method on the same table: `insert_many` takes the table lock and loops calling `insert`, which takes the SAME table lock again. A plain `Lock` would deadlock on that second acquire by the same thread; an `RLock` lets the owning thread re-enter and only fully releases when the outermost `with` exits."

> ❓ "Why per-table, not one global lock?"
> "One global lock is correct but serializes the whole database — inserts into `users` would block inserts into `orders` even though they share nothing. Per-table striping lets different tables proceed in parallel; only same-table writers serialize. And I keep validation outside the lock so type-checking never holds it."

> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?"
> "Deadlock needs two locks acquired in different orders. Here a single operation only ever holds ONE table lock — there's no nested acquisition of a *second* table's lock — so there's no lock-ordering cycle. The one re-entry (`insert_many` → `insert`) is the same thread re-taking the same lock, which `RLock` handles."

> ❓ "This is read-heavy — would a read-write lock help?"
> "If reads dominated and didn't mutate, a read-write lock would let many `select`s run in parallel and only block on writes. It's a clean upgrade because `select` is genuinely read-only here. I'd reach for it if profiling showed read contention; for v1 the simple `RLock` keeps the reasoning trivial."

> ❓ "Why lock `select` at all if it's just reading?"
> "Without it, a `select` iterating the row map can collide with a concurrent `insert` mutating that dict — `RuntimeError: dict changed size during iteration`. `lookup` already returns a copy of a bucket, but the row-id walk needs the lock (or a snapshot). The RLock is the simplest correct choice."

> ❓ "What if the lock-holder crashes mid-critical-section?"
> "In-process, a crash takes the whole process down, so the half-done state dies with it — no orphaned lock. In the distributed DB version, the insert + index update is one transaction, so it either commits fully or not at all — no partial row, no lock to release."

❓ **IF ASKED — the thread-safe version.**

**Part A — the no-op hook + subclass:**
```python
class ThreadSafeDatabaseService(DatabaseService):
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        self._table_locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}
        self._map_lock = threading.Lock()     # guards the dict of per-table locks

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, table_name: str) -> Iterator[None]:
        # double-lock idiom: lock the MAP of locks first (two threads creating the
        # same key at once could corrupt the dict), grab the per-table lock,
        # then release the map lock before doing real work.
        with self._map_lock:
            lock = self._table_locks.setdefault(table_name, threading.RLock())
        with lock:                            # RLock: insert_many re-enters per row
            yield
```
- **`RLock` not `Lock`** — `insert_many` holds the table lock and calls `insert`, which re-acquires it.
- **Per-resource not global** — different tables write in parallel.
- **Double-lock idiom** — lock the dict of locks first to create the per-table lock safely, then hold only the table lock.

**Part B — the EXACT modified method.** The beauty of the Template-Method hook is that `insert`'s body is *identical*; only `_lock`'s behavior changed. To make the diff explicit:
```python
# ─── §6 version (clean, single-threaded) ───────────────────────────────────
def insert(self, table: str, values: dict) -> int:
    target = self._table(table)
    normalized = target.schema.validate_row(values)   # type check BEFORE locking
    with self._lock(table):           # _lock is a NO-OP here → no real mutual exclusion
        return target.insert(normalized)   # _next_id += 1; _rows[rid]=...; index.add(...)

# ─── §9 version (thread-safe) — diff: ZERO lines in insert; only _lock changed ──
def insert(self, table: str, values: dict) -> int:
    target = self._table(table)
    normalized = target.schema.validate_row(values)   # same as §6 — reads immutable schema
    with self._lock(table):           # now a per-table RLock → the block below is ATOMIC
        return target.insert(normalized)   # increment + store + index now one atomic step
```
🎙️ "The only diff from §6 to §9 is the *behavior* of `_lock` — the `insert` body doesn't change a character. Validation stays outside because it reads the immutable schema. Inside the lock: `target.insert` does increment-store-index as one atomic unit. The Template-Method hook means §6 never changes; I just override `_lock` in the subclass."

**Part C — prove it with a concurrency test** (real, in the suite, green):
```python
def test_concurrent_inserts_lose_no_rows_and_keep_index_consistent():
    db = ThreadSafeDatabaseService()
    db.create_table("events", [Column("id", INT), Column("name", STR), Column("bucket", INT)])
    db.create_index("events", "bucket")
    n, buckets = 50, 10
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)
    errors = []
    def worker(i):
        barrier.wait()                       # all 50 hit insert at the same instant
        try: db.insert("events", {"id": i, "name": f"e{i}", "bucket": i % buckets})
        except Exception as exc: errors.append(exc)
    threads = [threading.Thread(target=worker, args=(i,)) for i in range(n)]
    for t in threads: t.start()
    for t in threads: t.join()
    assert not errors
    assert db.row_count("events") == n            # (1) NO rows lost — exact count
    seen = 0
    for b in range(buckets):
        via_index = {r["id"] for r in db.select("events", Eq("bucket", b))}
        via_scan  = {r["id"] for r in db.select("events", Range("bucket", b, b))}
        assert via_index == via_scan              # (2) index EXACTLY equals scan
        seen += len(via_index)
    assert seen == n
```
🎙️ "The Barrier makes all 50 threads hit `insert` at the same instant — without it they'd take turns and no real race would surface. After joining: exactly 50 rows (no lost update), and for every bucket the index lookup equals the full scan (no drift). That's the proof — both the count AND the index-vs-scan equality, not just the count."

**Edge cases handled:**
- Wrong type / `bool` in INT → `SchemaError`, row not stored → `test_insert_rejects_wrong_type`, `..._bool_in_int_column`.
- Missing / extra column → `SchemaError` → `test_insert_rejects_missing_and_unknown_columns`.
- Empty schema → `SchemaError` → `test_empty_schema_rejected`.
- Unknown table → `TableNotFoundError` → `test_unknown_table_rejected`.
- Index on unknown / already-indexed column → `ColumnNotFoundError` / `IndexExistsError` → `test_index_on_unknown_column_rejected`, `test_duplicate_index_rejected`.
- Predicate / projection on unknown column → `ColumnNotFoundError` → `test_predicate_on_unknown_column_rejected`, `test_projection_on_unknown_column_rejected`.
- `Range` with an index present → still scans → `test_range_forces_scan_even_when_indexed`.
- Concurrent inserts (same table) → no loss + index consistent → the Barrier test; (different tables) → run in parallel → `test_concurrent_inserts_into_different_tables_dont_block`.

**Extensibility — interviewer twists.**

**(i) Level-calibration:** A mid-level candidate gets one or two of these; a senior gets several with depth. Each below is a *minimal* change because the `Predicate`/`Index` seams are additive.

**(ii) Summary table**

| Want to add | How (one class/method) | What changes in the core |
|---|---|---|
| `OR` queries | new `Or(Predicate)` with `matches = any(...)`, `eq_conditions=[]` | nothing |
| `IN` lookup | new `In(Predicate)`; `eq_conditions` can emit one value if it helps | nothing |
| Range-capable index | new `SortedIndex(Index)` + a `RangePredicate.range_conditions()` hook | one line in the planner to consult range conditions |
| Composite index | new `CompositeHashIndex` keyed on a tuple; planner reads multi-col equalities | one branch in `_candidate_rids` |
| Result streaming | `select` returns an iterator (yield projected rows) | one method body |

**(iii) Twist walkthroughs**

> **Twist 1 — "Add `OR` support."**
> Today the only composite is `And`. `OR` is the symmetric Composite.
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "It's a new `Predicate` subclass — `Or` whose `matches` is `any(...)` and whose `columns` is the union. It advertises no `eq_conditions` because an OR can't ride a single index lookup, so the planner cleanly falls back to a scan. Zero engine changes."
> **The change:**
> ```python
> class Or(Predicate):
>     def __init__(self, *parts): self.parts = parts
>     def matches(self, v): return any(p.matches(v) for p in self.parts)
>     def columns(self): return set().union(*(p.columns() for p in self.parts))
>     # no eq_conditions → planner scans (correct: OR can't use one equality bucket)
> ```
> **Trade-off:** `OR` always scans in v1. If a hot `OR` of equalities mattered, I'd union the per-branch index lookups — more planner logic, worth it only when measured.

> **Twist 2 — "Make a range query use an index."**
> The hash index can't answer ranges; that's the honest limitation I flagged.
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "I swap in a sorted index behind the same `Index` interface. I add a `range_conditions()` method to predicates (default empty), and one branch in the planner: if a range condition names a sorted-indexed column, do an O(log n + k) range scan instead of a full O(n) scan. `HashIndex` is untouched; `Table` is untouched."
> **The change:**
> ```python
> class SortedIndex(Index):           # backed by a balanced tree / SortedDict
>     def range(self, low, high) -> set[int]: ...   # O(log n + k)
> # planner: for col,(lo,hi) in predicate.range_conditions():
> #              ix = target.index_for(col)
> #              if isinstance(ix, SortedIndex): return ix.range(lo,hi), f"range({col})"
> ```
> **Trade-off:** a sorted index costs O(log n) writes vs O(1) for hash; worth it only on columns you actually range-query.

> **Twist 3 — "Support a composite index on (city, age)."**
> Single-column hash only, today.
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "A `CompositeHashIndex` keyed on a tuple `(city, age)`. The planner gains one check: if the predicate carries equalities on ALL of a composite index's columns, build the tuple key and do one O(1) lookup. Single-column behavior is unchanged; this is an added branch, not a rewrite."
> **The change:**
> ```python
> class CompositeHashIndex(Index):
>     def __init__(self, columns: tuple[str, ...]): self.columns = columns; self._b = {}
>     def add(self, key: tuple, rid): self._b.setdefault(key, set()).add(rid)
>     # planner: if {c for c,_ in eqs} ⊇ set(ix.columns): key = tuple(eq_map[c] ...)
> ```
> **Trade-off:** composite indexes only help when the query pins every indexed column; otherwise they're dead weight. Add them for known multi-column access patterns.

**Hardest follow-up questions:**

> ❓ "Hardest concurrency one: what if `update` and `select` race on the same table?"
> "Both take the same per-table `RLock`, so they fully serialize — a `select` either sees the row before or after the `update`, never mid-move between index buckets. The index `remove`+`add` inside `update` is within the critical section, so there's no instant where the index is missing the row in both buckets from a reader's view."

> ❓ "Distributed: what if you run multiple app servers over a shared store?"
> "My Python `RLock` only guards one process. I'd move the source of truth to a DB: row ids from a DB sequence, and each insert/update as one transaction so the row write and index (DB index) commit atomically. Cross-server atomicity becomes the DB's job; my service layer keeps the same API and drops the in-process lock."

> ❓ "Scale: 10M rows, frequent range queries?"
> "The hash index stays O(1) for equality, but `Range` is O(n) = 10M per scan — that's the hot spot. I'd add the `SortedIndex` from Twist 2 so ranges become O(log n + k). Storage-wise, `_rows` as one dict is fine at 10M in memory; beyond RAM I'd partition by a key or page to disk — but that crosses into 'real database' territory."

> ❓ "Undo: what if a multi-row `update` fails halfway?"
> "Today `update` mutates rows one by one, so a mid-loop exception leaves some rows changed — not atomic. To make it all-or-nothing I'd capture each row's old `(value, index-bucket)` before changing it and roll back on exception, or stage changes and commit at the end. That's the transaction-log extension point I deliberately left as a seam — the mutation methods already funnel through `Table`, so wrapping them is localized."

🎙️ **Final verbal summary:** "A tiny in-memory RDBMS where the key insight is that the `Table` owns both rows and indexes, so consistency is impossible to get wrong, and each `Predicate` advertises its own indexable equalities, so the planner picks index-vs-scan with three lines. The orchestrator coordinates through two Strategy seams — `Predicate` (Eq/Range/And) and `Index` (HashIndex) — so new operators and index types are new classes, never core edits. The one concurrency risk is a lost-update race in `insert`; I guard it with a per-table reentrant lock via a Template-Method hook, proved by a 50-thread Barrier test that asserts no rows lost AND index exactly equals scan. For distributed scale, I push id generation and atomic index updates down to the database with a sequence and a single transaction."
